title: Béchamel, the basic white sauce
subtitle: A roux, a slow pour of warm milk, a whisk that doesn't stop.
excerpt: A roux, a slow pour of warm milk, a whisk that doesn't stop. The white sauce that becomes Mornay, Soubise, mustard, parsley, or cheese with one or two extra ingredients.
sourceNotes:
Cross-referenced from three British and French nineteenth- and early-twentieth-century works, all in the public domain:

• Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), Section II Sauces, sauce béchamel. English translation by H. L. Cracknell and R. J. Kaufmann (Heinemann, 1907 edition referenced).
• Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), chapter on English sauces (Project Gutenberg).
• Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Chapter 4 on sauces (Project Gutenberg #10136).

The ratio (40 g butter to 40 g flour to 600 ml milk) is closer to Escoffier than to Beeton, whose white sauce is leaner. The technique notes follow Escoffier's instruction to scald the milk and to cook the flour out before serving.

content.text: Béchamel is the easiest of the five French
mother sauces

to learn first, and the one you will reach for most. It is the sauce in cauliflower cheese, in moussaka, in fish pie, in the lasagna layer between the meat and the pasta. The packet version on the supermarket shelf is the same idea, drier, with stabilisers.

Made properly, it takes about twenty minutes from cold pan to finished sauce. The ingredients fit on one shelf: butter, flour, milk, salt, white pepper, and a piece of nutmeg. The whole tutorial is really about three things, the temperature of the milk, the colour of the roux, and the rhythm of the whisk.
Get those right and every other white sauce in the French repertoire follows. Stir in cheese and mustard for Mornay. Onion and butter for Soubise. The base is the same.

attrs.body: Escoffier codified five base sauces in 1903: béchamel (milk), velouté (white stock), espagnole (brown stock), hollandaise (butter and egg yolk), and tomate (tomato). Every classical French sauce from supreme to mornay to bordelaise is a derivative of one of those five, one or two extra ingredients added to a finished base. Learn the bases, and the derivatives cost you almost no extra technique.
attrs.title: What 'mother sauce' actually means

content.text: What you need

items.name: Whole milk
items.substitutions: semi-skimmed at a push, never skimmed

items.name: White pepper
items.substitutions: black pepper if it's what you have, accepting visible flecks

attrs.quote: Béchamel, the most important of all white sauces, the basis of many, and itself a finished sauce of great delicacy.

content.text: Method
Step 1. Scald the milk
Pour the milk into the small pan over a low heat. If you have a bay leaf, an onion quarter, and a couple of cloves, stud the onion with the cloves and drop the lot in. You are going to
scald

the milk rather than boil it: heat it until you see small bubbles around the rim and the surface trembles, then cut the heat. About 80°C if you have a thermometer to hand. Steam is rising. The skin has not formed.
The point of warm milk is the next step. Cold milk hitting a hot roux is the single most common reason for lumps.

attrs.body: Bay leaf, onion, and cloves perfume the milk while it cools and lift the finished sauce out of the dairy register without anyone tasting onion. Strain them out before you whisk the milk into the roux. The classical version uses a piece of veal as well, skip that unless you happen to have some.
attrs.title: Aromatics in the milk

content.text: Step 2. Make the roux
Melt the butter in the bigger saucepan over a low heat. As soon as the foam has subsided, tip in the flour and stir with the wooden spoon. You have made a
roux
. Cook it gently for two or three minutes, stirring constantly. The paste will loosen, smell biscuity, and stay pale, a sort of magnolia colour. That is what you want.

attrs.body: A roux that browns past pale gold is a different sauce. Brown roux belongs in gravy and in espagnole; in a béchamel it shows as a beige streak in finished ivory and a faintly nutty taste where you wanted milky neutrality. If yours starts colouring, lift the pan off the heat.
attrs.title: Keep the heat moderate

content.text: Step 3. Combine
Strain the warm milk to remove the aromatics. Now swap the wooden spoon for the whisk.

Pour in about a third of the milk and whisk hard. The roux will seize into a thick paste, then loosen as you whisk. Pour in another third and whisk again, this time to a thick sauce. Pour in the last of the milk and whisk until everything is smooth and pourable. Lumps form when one of these stages gets rushed: a slow, three-stage pour is faster in the end than starting again.

attrs.body: First third, whisk to a paste. Second third, whisk to a sauce. Last third, whisk to smooth. Three stages, not one continuous pour. Each pause lets the starch hydrate before the next addition arrives.
attrs.title: The rhythm

content.text: Step 4. Cook out the flour

Bring the sauce up to a bare simmer and hold it there for five to eight minutes, whisking every minute or so to stop it catching on the bottom. The texture thickens as the starch grains absorb water and burst open. You are aiming for
nappe
: dip the back of a wooden spoon, draw a finger across the back, and the line should hold.

The taste shifts as it cooks. A raw sauce tastes faintly of paste; a properly cooked one tastes of warm milk with a soft thickness behind it. If you want to hold the sauce while something else finishes cooking, sit the pan in a
bain-marie
and press a sheet of buttered baking parchment against the surface to stop a skin forming.
Step 5. Season and finish

Pull the pan off the heat. Salt to taste. Grind in white pepper. Grate a small drift of nutmeg over the top, stir, and taste again. Nutmeg is the one classical seasoning that lifts béchamel from neutral to alive; too much makes it medicinal.

attrs.body: Appearance. Béchamel should be ivory. Black flecks read as scorched even when nothing is. If white pepper is not in the cupboard, use black and trust your eaters to forgive you.
attrs.title: Why white pepper, not black

content.text: Troubleshooting

attrs.intro: Almost every problem here is recoverable.

items.fix: Press the sauce through a fine sieve into a clean pan. Warm gently while whisking, and it will smooth out. Next time, scald the milk first and pour it in three stages.
items.cause: Cold milk hit hot roux, or the whisk stopped.

items.fix: Simmer for another five minutes on a low heat, whisking. The taste cooks out within a few minutes.
items.cause: The roux was undercooked, or the finished sauce did not simmer long enough.

items.fix: Pour the unscorched top into a clean pan without scraping the burnt bottom. Continue on a lower heat. Bin the burnt pan-bottom rather than working it back in.
items.cause: Heat too high, or the sauce sat without stirring.

items.fix: Whisk in more warm milk a splash at a time until you hit a consistency you like. Re-season.
items.cause: Too much flour, or the simmer reduced it past where you wanted.

items.fix: Simmer for longer, whisking. If it still hasn't thickened after another ten minutes, make a small second roux in a separate pan (10 g butter, 10 g flour, cook to magnolia) and whisk it through.
items.cause: Under-floured roux, or the sauce did not simmer long enough for the starch to gel.

attrs.heading: When it goes wrong

content.text: Variations

attrs.intro: Each is the same béchamel with one or two extra ingredients stirred through the finished sauce.

items.name: Mornay
items.description: Whisk in 80 g grated Gruyère and 20 g grated Parmesan off the heat. A splash of cream loosens it. The sauce in cauliflower cheese.

items.name: Soubise
items.description: Sweat 250 g finely sliced onion in butter until soft and sweet but without colour. Blend smooth, then fold into the finished béchamel. Classical with roast lamb or with eggs.

items.name: Mustard
items.description: Whisk in a tablespoon of English or Dijon mustard once the sauce is off the heat. Sharp counter to white fish, smoked haddock, or grilled mackerel.

items.name: Parsley
items.description: Stir in three tablespoons of finely chopped flat-leaf parsley right before serving. The classical sauce for boiled bacon and broad beans.

items.name: Cheese
items.description: The plainer English cousin of Mornay: 100 g grated mature Cheddar stirred through the finished béchamel, a pinch of mustard powder, salt to balance. The sauce on a cauliflower cheese as you remember it from school dinners, only better.

attrs.label: the derivatives
attrs.heading: Five sauces from this one

content.text: Sub-tutorials
Where this sauce lives

Béchamel turns up everywhere British home cookery meets French technique. It is the white layer between the meat and the pasta sheets in lasagna alla bolognese, the cheese sauce on cauliflower cheese, the binding in moussaka above the lamb, the silk underneath the topping of a fish pie.

Once it sits comfortably under your hand, you stop following the recipe and start adjusting it: a little more nutmeg for one dish, no nutmeg at all for another, a flatter sauce for lasagna and a richer one for fish pie. That moment is the point of learning the base.

attrs.label: kit recommendation
attrs.title: Balloon whisk, 25 cm
attrs.description: Stainless wires set into a stainless handle. Heavy enough to feel like a tool, light enough to spend twenty minutes whisking with. Stays straight after a year of dishwashers.
